perturbation.original_text: Deducting the dues of Party A, Party B should pay Party A the energy saving service fee of the last month before the 15t h of each month.
perturbation.changed_text: Deducting the dues of Party A, Party B should pay Party A the energy saving service fee of the last month before the last day of the following month.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the payment deadline from the 15th of the month to the last day of the following month creates a potentially unreasonable delay in payment, which may not be compliant with typical commercial payment terms and could violate regulations requiring timely payment for services rendered. Although the contract does not explicitly state which laws govern it, the delay could violate prompt payment laws generally.
perturbation.law_explanation: While 31 U.S. Code § 3905 primarily concerns federal government payments, it establishes a principle of timely payment. Delaying payment until the end of the following month might be considered an unreasonably long delay, especially if it deviates significantly from industry standards. The modified text contradicts the general principle of timely payment for services rendered. Although the original contract doesn't violate this law, the change creates concern.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 5 CFR § 1315.8 - Rebates. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 5 CFR § 1315.8 - Rebates. CFR prev | next § 1315.8 Rebates. Agencies shall determine governmentwide commercial purchase card payment dates based on an analysis of the total costs and total benefits to the Federal government as a whole, unless specified in a contract . When calculating costs and benefits, agencies are expected to include the cost to the government of paying early. This cost is the interest the government would have earned, at the Current Value of Funds rate, for each day that payment was not made. Agencies may factor in benefits gained from paying early due to, for example, streamlining the payment process or other efficiencies. A rebate formula is provided in § 1315.17 and at the Prompt Payment website at www.fms.treas.gov/prompt/index.html. CFR Toolbox Law about... perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a potentially unreasonable delay in payment by extending the deadline to the last day of the following month. This contradicts the general principle of timely payment for services rendered, which is reflected in 31 U.S. Code § 3905 (Snippet 1), even though that law primarily concerns federal government payments, it sets a standard for prompt payment. The original contract, specifying payment by the 15th of the month, aligns more closely with this principle than the modified version. The change weakens the obligation for timely payment, which can create legal problems.

perturbation.original_text: If the project was delayed due to Party A, Party B has right to dispose the equipment on the construction site after two month past due date.
perturbation.changed_text: If the project was delayed due to Party A, Party B has right to dispose of the equipment on the construction site after 15 days past due date.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the grace period before Party B can dispose of Party A's equipment from two months to 15 days could be considered an unreasonably short timeframe, potentially violating due process or breach of contract principles requiring a reasonable opportunity to cure a default. A shorter grace period before exercising remedies may be viewed as commercially unreasonable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of Contract and Cure Period Principles
perturbation.law_citation: Uniform Commercial Code § 2-609. Right to Adequate Assurance of Performance
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-609 allows a party, when reasonable grounds for insecurity arise, to demand adequate assurance of due performance and, until such assurance is received, suspend performance. While this section deals with the sale of goods, the underlying principle of requiring a reasonable opportunity to provide assurance or cure a breach applies to general contract law. Reducing the disposal timeframe to 15 days could be seen as denying Party A a reasonable opportunity to remedy the delay or provide assurance, potentially violating the spirit of contract law and fair dealing. This is because 15 days may not be enough time for the breaching party to correct their wrongdoings.

View more information about the ALI collections The National Bankruptcy Archives (NBA) In 2000, the Biddle Law Library and the American College of Bankruptcy collaborated to establish the National Bankruptcy Archives (NBA), a national repository of materials relating to the history of debtor-creditor relations, bankruptcy, and the reorganization of debt. The NBA currently contains over 1,000 linear feet of personal papers and institutional records. View more information about the NBA collections Explore the NBA Oral History collections Biddle Collections The Collections consist of manuscript collections, rare books, oral histories, and a select number of Penn Carey Law School’s records. The manuscript collections include the papers of George Wharton Pepper, Bernie Segal, William Draper Lewis, and David L. Bazelon. The Raymond F. Trent Collection consists of books, periodicals, articles, and audio tapes concerning the history of education and practice of Black lawyers in the United States. Some records relating to the operations of Penn Carey Law School are maintained by the University Archives . Please contact us for more information. View more information about the Biddle Law Library Collections Explore the collection of The Report the Law School’s yearbook Explore the Penn Carey Law Oral History Project collection Contact & Hours The Archives and Special Collections office is located on the fourth floor of Tanenbaum Hall. The mailing address is: University of Pennsylvania Carey Law School Biddle Law Library - Archives Department 3501 Sansom Street Philadelphia, PA 19104-3406 For questions about the Archives, the Rare Books Collection, or other special collections, please email the Archives Department at biddlearchives@law.upenn.edu . The Archives is open Tuesday-Friday, 10AM to 4PM. Appointments should be made 2 weeks in advance of the visit. Visitors will be asked to show identification. Policies Registration All researchers are asked to fill out our Researcher Registration Form. This form can be filled out prior to your visit or on the day of your arrival. Archives - Researcher Registration Form On-site Research Items in the Archives do not circulate. If you wish to come to the Archives to conduct research, you may do so free of charge. However, you must contact the department ahead of time to schedule an appointment. Appointments should be made 2 weeks in advance of desired research date. Visitors to the Law School will be asked to show identification. Remote Research For patrons who cannot travel to the Law School to conduct research, the Archives will assist in the identification of potentially relevant materials for your research topic or for photocopying. We do not transcribe, paraphrase, or otherwise interpret information for the researcher. Research requests generally take 2-3 weeks to complete, depending on the nature of the question and the number of outstanding requests at the time of submission. In addition, the Archives can only offer one hour of research assistance, patrons are asked to make an appointment for requests that will take longer than one hour to complete. Please also note that remote research requests are limited, and the Archives can only handle copying/scanning requests up to 20 individual documents. If a request exceeds this number, we suggest you schedule an appointment to visit the Archives in person. Handling Materials Wash hands before touching materials. Protective gloves may be required. Remove only one folder from a box at a time. Documents should be maintained in the exact order they are arranged in each file. If a document is out of order, notify the Archives staff. Make no marks or erasures on any document. Use strips provided to mark pages for copying. Eating or drinking is not permitted. Use pencils only, no pens or markers. Laptop
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the time frame for Party B to dispose of Party A's equipment from two months to 15 days. This change creates a legal contradiction because the reduced timeframe could be deemed unreasonable, potentially violating due process or principles of fair dealing, as it may not provide Party A a reasonable opportunity to remedy the delay or provide assurance. The law explanation supports this, stating that reducing the disposal timeframe to 15 days could be seen as denying Party A a reasonable opportunity to remedy the delay or provide assurance, potentially violating the spirit of contract law and fair dealing. Since the changed contract setting violates state law about reasonable time frames, it falls under inconsistencies - legal contradiction. The snippets are not helpful in determining the degree to which the time frame is unreasonable.
